display_name: High memory utilization on a Linux-based NOS is usually buffers/cache, not a leak
context: Reviewing memory utilization on a modern Linux-based network OS and seeing total memory usage near 80-95%
observation: Reported memory utilization is high and 'free' memory is very low
naive_interpretation: The device has a memory leak and is about to run out of memory or crash
correct_interpretation: Linux-based NOSes (SONiC, EOS, NX-OS, IOS-XR) deliberately use otherwise-idle RAM for page cache and buffers, so 'free' memory is expected to be low on a healthy system. The kernel reclaims cache instantly under pressure. The number that matters is available memory (free + reclaimable cache) and whether the system is actually under pressure — swapping, OOM-killer events, or a single process whose RSS grows monotonically. High total utilization with healthy available memory and no OOM activity is normal steady state.
why: The Linux kernel caches file/page data in free RAM to speed I/O and frees it on demand, so low 'free' is by design, not exhaustion
The meaningful metric is MemAvailable (free plus reclaimable), not 'free' alone
True exhaustion shows up as swap-in/out activity, OOM-killer log entries, or a specific process RSS climbing without bound
A leak is identified by tracking a single process's memory growth over time, not by the system-wide percentage

confirmation_commands.purpose: Read 'available' (not just 'free') and check whether swap is in use to judge real pressure
Show used/free/available memory; compare available against demand rather than reacting to used percentage
Identify whether a single process RSS is growing over time, which distinguishes a leak from normal cache use

traps: Reading 'free' memory instead of 'available' and concluding the device is out of memory
Calling normal page-cache usage a leak without trending a specific process over time
Ignoring the real exhaustion signals — swap activity and OOM-killer events — that actually indicate trouble
Rebooting to 'recover' memory that the kernel would have reclaimed automatically under pressure
